moment_text: Well, what's interesting in my case is that I did not feel anything in my breast. This is. Breast cancer was not something I was thinking about. I was only in my thirties. I was, uh, 36 years old. And the way that my cancer was found the first time was because I went into my gynecologist. Uh, she was just the gynecologist that had been recommended to me. Um, and, um, I, I'd been seeing her for a couple years. I was now in my mid thirties, and she said that she liked to offer her patients in their mid thirties, uh, what she called a baseline mammogram. So she wasn't saying, okay, we're gonna start doing mammograms every year or whatever at 35, 36. She was like, let's do one if we can compare it in the future. And I had really gone in to get birth control. I wanted like a Nova Ring prescription or something like that. And she just offered me this service. And I was a ur, I'm a journalist. The trained journalist, I worked as a journalist for a long time, and so I thought, you know, I, I want information. And I said, well, sure. You're offering me the chance to be screened early once. Uh, for, for, for, for, so we can compare it later to to later mammograms. I'm gonna take this opportunity to get more information. So I had no idea. I would actually be diagnosed with, uh, what my doctor called extensive DCIS, which is, uh, ductal carcinoma in C two, which means, or in situ two, which means the can, there are cancer cells, but they are within the milk ducts. So they're not yet invasive. They're not yet. Uh, something that could spread out of the breast. Uh, they were called stage zero, and on my mammogram they looked like calcification. So like, like someone took a key and like scratched, you know, the slide? Yeah. The sort of x-ray from the mammogram. They look, that's what they looked like and I, I learned that those were calcifications and signs of dying cells and, and indications of cancer. So that's how it came about for me. I didn't feel anything. It was just something that someone offered me and I took it.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2 with agency signals (seeking_care, seeking_information) and a non-none system interaction (healthcare_provider), detailing an incidental but crucial pathway to diagnosis.

moment_text: I really was going through a lot at this time. I mean, I, I was in love, I was looking for birth control, so I had a good relationship going. But my mother was very, very sick. Uh, at this point. She was being fe, she, she had a feeding tube, you know, she was bedridden. I was. Really otherwise focused. And I just remember my doctor saying, okay, it's DCIS. It's technically stage zero. You can't die of it because it's, it hasn't, it hasn't become invasive. It hasn't spread beyond the milk ducks. But I had relatively small breasts, and she called it extensive, which meant it was in sort of a quadrant, not just one little area. So it seemed to me that it was already beginning to spread, but mostly I thought that if I acted super aggressively. I could get rid of this problem once and for all, you know, claim the victory and, and move on with my life. And so, um, I knew that DCIS was cancerous. I also knew that they don't, I, it was, as it was explained to me, they, there was no, there was no option saying, let's just watch this. I later learned that a lot of that has to do with science. We don't. Not everyone knows which DCIS cases will turn into invasive. Certainly some do, but not all do. And so basically when someone is diagnosed with DCIS, at least at that time, for me, uh, the recommendation was to act on it. Plus the fact that I was young, younger than the usual age that people think about breast cancer and certainly younger than screening age, um, regular screening age. Um. So, because I was young and black and, and I, my doctor felt that cancers can be more aggressive in black women. We decided to be very aggressive. I thought, I thought I was being very aggressive. I had a double mastectomy. Now I didn't have radiation, which is something that I, in retrospect, wish I had done because maybe that would've prevented the recurrence. Um, but I didn't feel like, I mean, the, the shortest way of answering that is to say that I didn't feel like. I had a choice of that. I was gonna sit there and watch it, but I also had no interest in sitting and watching it because it, for me, cancer is cancer. Whether it was in the milk ducks or outside the milk ducks. I wanted to get, get rid of it, get, get it outta my body, focus on my mother, um, and, and be healed forever. I thought.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2 with emotional signals (overwhelm), agency signals (making_decisions), and barrier signals (lack_of_information) in a healthcare context, highlighting the complex factors influencing treatment decisions.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker mentions being Black as a factor in the decision to be aggressive, which could be a signal for a theme related to racial disparities in healthcare, but it's presented as a collaborative decision point with her doctor rather than a barrier.

moment_text: Well, I think this is maybe a throwaway, but it shouldn't be. The whole point is to get to that after diagnosis, right? So I think that as young as you are when you are diagnosed, and as much as it may feel like why me and woe is me? It is a good thing to be catching it and to be under increased surveillance. I mean, it's not good to have developed breast cancer, but if you're gonna have developed it, it's good that you caught it and now you're gonna, people are, your, your caregivers are gonna be paying a lot more attention to, to that for the rest of your life. And so I think that you gotta take that as a win because the goal is to live.

moment_text: Another thing to keep in mind is that you are not alone. There is, I personally, this is my personal experience, but I feel like. Even, you know, it's kinda like better late than never even connecting later years after I was going through the, the, the hardest parts of my, I was actually going through active treatment. It's still been helpful for me because it's helpful for me to be helpful to others. It's helpful for me to kind of see other people thriving, even. You know, if, even though I might have some anxieties about cancer returning, I see people thriving with, you know, more advanced stages than I, than I, than I had.
eligibility_rationale: This moment contains strong emotional signals (connection), agency signals (seeking_support), and a barrier signal (social_isolation) within a peer_community interaction, meeting the evidence gate A2.

moment_text: Um, I think that, you know, it's important to know that you're not alone. And then lastly, I think you have to advocate for yourself.
